chunk_summary: Guidance for managing uncomplicated SAM when outpatient therapeutic feeding programs are unavailable, emphasizing structured feeding counseling, follow-up, and risk-based inpatient admission thresholds.
learning_objectives: Understand the management of SAM without access to outpatient therapeutic feeding programs.
Learn structured feeding counseling techniques using locally available energy-dense foods.
Identify risk factors for inpatient admission in severe malnutrition cases.
Recognize the importance of early follow-up and written return precautions for higher-risk outpatient cases.
key_facts: Uncomplicated SAM cases without OTP/CMAM should be labeled as 'higher-risk outpatient' and require structured feeding counseling.
Weekly early reviews are clinically defensible for severe anthropometric deficits.
Written return precautions include symptoms like vomiting, fever, lethargy, worsening cough, diarrhea, or new oedema.
Outpatient failure in severe malnutrition can be rapid; inpatient admission thresholds should be lower when follow-up reliability is low.
Community-based care must complement inpatient services, with implementation varying across states and districts.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Facility-based guidance recognizes that community-based care must complement inpatient services, but implementation varies across states and districts. (Reference 1)
document_key_topics: Management of SAM without outpatient therapeutic feeding programs
Structured feeding counseling using locally available foods
Importance of follow-up and return precautions in malnutrition care
